The Boston Celtics may have gotten to a place where they wouldn’t mind seeing Kyrie Irving depart this offseason. At this point, it would be hard to blame them.

The Celtics have fallen into a 3-1 hole in their playoff series against the Milwaukee Bucks. Irving is playing poorly — really poorly — hitting 7 of 22 from the field in Game 4 and 8 of 22 in Game 3. That cold streak wouldn’t justify the ousting of Irving, except that Irving has bungled the situation. And he tends to bungle tough situations.

“He’s a volume-usage player. He likes to dribble the ball a lot. He likes to shoot the ball a lot. And that’s who he has become. Unfortunately for the Celtics, that’s not how they’re built, OK?” ESPN analyst and retired player Jalen Rose said. “This is the team that made it to the conference finals last year without him. So now that they insert him into the lineup, he’s got to be, ‘Make everybody better. Make everybody comfortable.’

“When he goes 7 for 22, you can’t turn around and go 8 for 22. You’ve got to get off the ball. Then when you go to the press conference and say, ‘I should’ve taken 30 shots,’ what do you think the other 14 guys in the car going home saying, ‘We can’t wait until this guy gets out of here. We’ll help him pack his bags.”



After Game 3, he said he didn’t think we’d see another 8-for-22 performance. When he was even worse in Game 4, he didn’t seem too upset with his shooting woes.

“Who cares?” Irving said on Monday night, adding later, “For me, the 22 shots, I should have shot 30 — really, I’m that great of a shooter.”

It’s easy to understand what Irving is saying: he’s an All-Star and a generational talent. It’s also easy to roll your eyes, point to the most recent statistics and think, really?

The question isn’t whether media members like Rose are rolling their eyes at Irving. Are his teammates? They might be fed up with the Irving circus, which has been a bit of a disaster in Boston, particularly this season when he sniped at younger players. He’s a moody player — as shown with his strange on-again, off-again relationship with LeBron James — who can completely disengage when things take a negative turn. No doubt, there were moments when Irving has contributed with positivity and leadership, mostly notably bringing his teammates together during one particularly rigorous road trip during the regular season. Those flashes of chemistry haven’t been enough to tie together a Celtics team that has been erratic as Kyrie’s mood — and maybe that’s no coincidence.

His show-stopping, ball-stopping play style may not fit in Boston. And even more important, his antics, even if they’re occasional, may not be what the Celtics need out of their No. 1 player.
